titleStmt.title: The British Ambassador to the Secretary of State.

head.hi: The British Ambassador to the Secretary of State.

div.p:
Dear Mr. Secretary: I beg to acknowledge with thanks your letter of the 14th instant in which you are good enough to enclose for my information a copy of a communication from General Carranza
in reply to your representations on behalf, as you were good enough to inform me, of a Spanish subject. I have lost no time in communicating copy of your letter and its enclosure to my Government. In the meanwhile I note that according to General Carranza’s statement you told him that countries which have recognised the Huerta Government can not apply to him since every nation can have but one diplomatic corps accredited, and that consequently those countries which have recognised Huerta can not make representations to him. He goes on to ask that should Consuls of the United States present unofficial representations to him in the name of foreign Governments they may be provided “with a power properly drawn up.”

I beg to observe that, as you are aware, the object of any such communication is humanity; that is, the immediate protection of life and property. The United States Government has, as I understand, no hesitation in applying to General Huerta with the object of obtaining protection for its citizens. In different parts of the world the British Government, as you are aware, has extended the good offices of its own Consuls to United States citizens when United States citizens were in danger and when there was no United States Consul on the spot. For this purpose no “power properly drawn up “has been considered necessary. With or without request from the United States Government the services of British Consuls have been invoked, and this is in accordance with the general international usage and the law of nations.

There is a British Vice-Consul at Chihuahua through whom representations have been made to General Villa on behalf both of British and Spanish subjects. But there is no other British Consular representative in the districts controlled by the Constitutionalist forces. In accordance with international usage I have appealed with success for the kind intervention of United States Consuls. Should the United States acquiesce in the new practice now demanded by General Carranza I have the honour to request that you will give me timely notice.

In this connection I beg to state that I have received from Mexico several reports based on good authority and worthy of credence. These reports are to the effect that the death of Benton, the refusal to surrender his body and the failure hitherto of the attempts to secure an open and impartial enquiry into the cause of his death—have seriously affected the security of foreign and especially of British subjects in the districts controlled by the Constitutionalist forces. I am also informed, on good authority, that Villa has openly expressed the most hostile sentiments to Great Britain. In view of the serious consequences which may follow I think it my duty to bring the above to the attention of the Government of the United States.
